item.title: Discovering Descriptors
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
This talk was preôÙsentôÙed at PyôÙCon CZ 2017 and EuôÙroPyôÙthon 2017. Here are the refôÙerôÙences and reôÙsources for the preôÙsenôÙtaôÙtion.

The code used for the talk, is availôÙable here and conôÙtains some exôÙplaôÙnaôÙtions along with the exôÙamôÙples.

Leer mûÀsãÎ (quedan 1 minutos de lectura)

item.title: The __set_name__ method for descriptors
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
Descriptors generally have to interact with attributes of the managed object, and this is done by inspecting __dict__ on that object (or calling getattr/setattr, but the problem is the same), and finding the key under the specific name.

For this reaôÙson, the deôÙscripôÙtor will have to know the name of the key to look for, which is reôÙlatôÙed to the name of the atôÙtribute is manôÙagôÙing.

On preôÙviôÙous verôÙsions of Python this had to be done exôÙplicôÙitôÙly. If we wantôÙed to work around it, there were some more adôÙvanced ways to do so. LuckôÙiôÙly, afôÙter PEP-487 (added in Python 3.6), there are some enôÙhanceôÙments reôÙgardôÙing class creôÙation, which alôÙso afôÙfects deôÙscripôÙtors.

Let's reôÙview the probôÙlem, the preôÙviôÙous apôÙproachôÙes to tackôÙle it, and the modôÙern way of solvôÙing it.

Leer mûÀsãÎ (quedan 4 minutos de lectura)

item.title: Descriptors & Decorators
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
DeôÙscripôÙtors are an amazôÙing tool to have in our toolôÙbox, as they come in handy in many opôÙporôÙtuôÙniôÙties.

ProbôÙaôÙbly the best thing about deôÙscripôÙtors, is that they can imôÙprove othôÙer soôÙluôÙtionôÙs. Let's see how we can write betôÙter decôÙoôÙraôÙtors, by usôÙing deôÙscripôÙtors.

Leer mûÀsãÎ (quedan 5 minutos de lectura)

item.title: Types of Descriptors
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
ReôÙsumôÙing from where we left ofôÙf, on the preôÙviôÙous post, on which we took A first look at deôÙscripôÙtors, it's time to exôÙplore their difôÙferôÙent types and how they work inôÙterôÙnalôÙly.

In Python, almost everything is represented with a dictionary. Objects are dictionaries. Classes are objects, hence they also are contained into a dictionary. This is denoted by the __dict__ attribute that objects have.

There are two types of descriptors: data descriptors and non-data ones. If a descriptor implements both 1 __get__() and __set__(), it's called a data descriptor; otherwise is a non-data descriptor.

Leer mûÀsãÎ (quedan 7 minutos de lectura)

item.title: A first look at descriptors
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
DeôÙscripôÙtors are one of the most powôÙerôÙful feaôÙtures of Python. The reaôÙson why they're so powôÙerôÙful is beôÙcause they enôÙable us to conôÙtrol the core opôÙerôÙaôÙtions (get, set, delete) 1, of an atôÙtribute in a givôÙen obôÙjecôÙt, so that we can hook a parôÙticôÙuôÙlar code, conôÙtrolled by us, in orôÙder to modôÙiôÙfy, change, or exôÙtend the origôÙiôÙnal opôÙerôÙaôÙtion.

Leer mûÀsãÎ (quedan 7 minutos de lectura)
